revision.text:
[[commons:Category:Ikaho-jinja]][[ja:伊香保神社]] {{Expand Japanese|伊香保神社}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Ikaho Shrine | native_name = 伊香保神社 | image = Ikaho-jinja 01.jpg | mapframe = yes | mapframe-zoom = 15 | mapframe-wikidata = yes | mapframe-point = none | map_type = Japan | map_caption = Ikaho Shrine | coordinates = {{coord|36.495916666666666|138.91583333333332}} | map_relief = 1 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | deity = {{ill|Ōkuninushi|lt=Ōkuninushi|ca|Ōkuninushi|en|Ōkuninushi|es|Ōkuninushi|fa|اوکونینوشی|fr|Ōkuninushi|hu|Ókuninusi|id|Ookuninushi|it|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主|ko|오오쿠니누시|mni|ꯑꯣꯀꯨꯅꯤꯅꯨꯁꯤ|pl|Ōkuninushi|pt|Ookuninushi|ru|Окунинуси|simple|Ōkuninushi|sr|Okuninuši|sv|Ōkuninushi|tr|Ōkuninushi|uk|Окунінуші|vi|Ōkuninushi|zh|大國主|qid=Q276944|1=Ōkuninushi}} | established = {'time': '+0825-00-00T00:00:00Z', 'timezone': 0, 'before': 0, 'after': 0, 'precision': 9, 'calendarmodel': 'http://www.wikidata.org/entity/Q1985786'} }} {{nihongo|'''Ikaho Shrine'''|伊香保神社}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Kōzuke Province]]. == instance of (P31) == * {{ill|Shinto shrine|lt=Shinto shrine|ar|جينجا|arz|ضريح شنتو|bcl|Shinto shrine|ca|Jinja|cs|Šintoistická svatyně|da|Shinto-tempel|de|Shintō-Schrein|el|Σιντοϊστικό Ιερό|en|Shinto shrine|eo|Ŝintoa sanktejo|es|Jinja|eu|Jinja|fa|معبد شینتویی|fi|Šintolainen pyhäkkö|fr|Sanctuaire shinto|hi|शिंतो xintoísta|ru|Дзиндзя|sc|Jinja|simple|Shinto shrine|sl|Šintoistično svetišče|sr|Шинтоистички храм|sv|Jinja|th|ศาลเจ้าชินโต|tr|Şinto tapınağı|uk|Святилище (синто)|vi|Thần 'after': 0, 'precision': 11, 'calendarmodel': 'http://www.wikidata.org/entity/Q1985727'} ** P1534: {{ill|Shinto Directive|lt=Shinto Directive|en|Shinto Directive|fa|بخشنامه شینتو|fr|Directive shinto|id|Pengarahan Shinto|ja|神道指令|zh|神道指令|qid=Q3029647|1=Shinto Directive}} == legal form (P1454) == * {{ill|Q7311343|ltq=Q7311343|qid=Q7311343|1=religious corporation}}https://www.houjin-bangou.nta.go.jp/download/zenken/index.html == country (P17) == * {{ill|Q17|ltq=Q17|qid=Q17|1=Japan}} == name in kana (P1814) == * * 377-0102https://www.houjin-bangou.nta.go.jp/download/zenken/index.html == TripAdvisor ID (P3134) == * 1384151 == Corporate Number (Japan) (P3225) == * 8070005005451 == nighttime view (P3451) == * Ikaho-jinja 03.jpg == Commons category (P373) == * Ikaho-jinja == said to be the same as (P460) == * {{ill|Q135039963|ltq=Q135039963|qid=Q135039963|1=Ikahono Shrine}} == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '群馬県渋川市伊香保町伊香保2', 'language': 'ja'} * {'text': '群馬県北群馬郡吉岡町大久保', 'language': 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Ikaho-jinja}} [[JA:伊香保神社]] {{wikidata link|Q17220841}} == Japanese content == {{Citation method|date=October 14, 2014 (Tue) 11:12 (UTC)}} {{Shinto shrine |Name=Ikaho Shrine |Location={{ill|lt=Gunma Prefecture|Q129499|qid=Q129499|1=Gunma Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Shibukawa City|Q851492|qid=Q851492|1=Shibukawa, Gunma}}{{ill|lt=Ikaho Town|Q1658038|qid=Q1658038|1=Ikaho, Gunma}} Ikaho 2 |Image=[[File:Ikaho-jinja 01.jpg|250px]]
Haiden (worship hall) |Coordinates={{Wiki coordinates 2-stage degrees minutes seconds|36|29|45.3|N|138|54|57|E|display=title,inline}} |Enshrined deity={{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|lt=Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|qid=Q276944|1=Ōkuninushi}}
{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sukunahikona-no-Mikoto|1=Sukunabikona|qid=Q3275687}} |Shrine rank={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|qid=Q134917286}} (Myōjin Taisha) · former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} |Founded={{ill|lt=Tencho era|Q1373138|qid=Q1373138|1=Tenchō}} 2 |Annual festival={{ill|lt=September 19|Q2896|qid=Q2896|1=September 19}} |Map= {{Location map|Japan Gunma|label=Ikaho Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Ikaho Shrine''' (いかほじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|qid=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|lt=Gunma Prefecture|Q129499|qid=Q129499|1=Gunma Prefecture}}, {{ill|lt=Shibukawa City|Q851492|qid=Q851492|1=Shibukawa, Gunma}}. It is a shrine that protects {{ill|lt=Ikaho Onsen|Q11381013|qid=Q11381013|1=Ikaho Onsen}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|qid=Q134917286}} ({{ill|lt=Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964|qid=Q9610964|1=Myōjin Taisha}}), and under the {{ill|lt=Modern system of shrine ranks|Q712534|qid=Q712534|lt=former shrine rank|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} it was both a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} and a {{ill|lt=Village shrine|Q134917316|qid=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}. It is regarded as the {{ill|lt=Ichinomiya|Q1656379|qid=Q1656379|lt=Sannomiya|1=Ichinomiya}} of {{ill|lt=Kōzuke Province|Q1054838|qid=Q1054838|1=Kōzuke Province}}. It is also written as Ikaho Shrine (伊加保神社). It is said that in the past the {{ill|lt=Sannomiya Shrine (Yoshioka Town)|Q21655265|qid=Q21655265|lt=Sannomiya Shrine|1=Sannomiya Shrine (Yoshioka Town)}} (Ōkubo, {{ill|lt=Kitagunma District|Q1075450|qid=Q1075450|1=Kitagunma District, Gunma}}, {{ill|lt=Yoshioka Town|Q1154580|qid=Q1154580|1=Yoshioka, Gunma}}) was the original main shrine. === Enshrined deities === The current principal {{ill|lt=enshrined deities|Q11591100|qid=Q11591100|1=Saijin}} are two deities: {{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|lt=Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|qid=Q276944|1=Ōkuninushi}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sukunahikona-no-Mikoto|1=Sukunabikona|qid=Q3275687}}, who are worshipped as gods of hot springs and medicine. However, before the shrine was relocated to the present hot spring area, it is said that a single deity known as “Ikatsubo no Kami,” associated with {{ill|lt=mountain worship|Q93697439|qid=Q93697439|1=Mountain worship}} of the mountains of “Ikaho” (the former name of this region including Mount Haruna), was enshrined. Another theory, considering the enshrined deity of Sannomiya Shrine, holds that it was {{ill|lt=Eleven-Faced Kannon|Q11404731|qid=Q11404731|1=Ekādaśamukha}}. === History === According to the shrine’s tradition at its current location within the hot spring town, it was founded in {{ill|lt=825|Q24624|qid=Q24624|1=825}} ({{ill|lt=Tencho era|Q1373138|qid=Q1373138|1=Tenchō}} 2). The Sannomiya Shrine, regarded as the former main shrine, was founded in {{ill|lt=750|Q23631|qid=Q23631|1=750}} ({{ill|lt=Tenpyō Shōhō|Q1373169|qid=Q1373169|1=Tenpyō-shōhō}} 2). Its first appearance in the {{ill|lt=Six National Histories|Q1151168|qid=Q1151168|1=Rikkokushi}} is in the {{ill|lt=Shoku Nihon Kōki|Q1041301|qid=Q1041301|1=Shoku Nihon Kōki}} entry for {{ill|lt=835|Q23894|qid=Q23894|1=835}} ({{ill|lt=Jōwa era (Japan)|Q1075605|qid=Q1075605|lt=Jōwa|1=Jōwa (Heian period)}} 2), 9th month, day of Kanmi, where it is listed as a Myōjin shrine. In the 6th year, 6th month, day of Kōshin, it was promoted to Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade, and thereafter rose in rank, becoming Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade on May 25, {{ill|lt=880|Q23618|qid=Q23618|1=880}} ({{ill|lt=Gangyō era|Q747451|qid=Q747451|1=Gangyō}} 4), alongside {{ill|lt=Akagi Shrine|Q11635468|qid=Q11635468|1=Akagi Shrines}}. In the Engishiki it is recorded as “Ikaho Shrine, Gunma District, Myōjin Taisha,” and was listed as a {{ill|lt=Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964|qid=Q9610964|1=Myōjin Taisha}}. In the “Kōzuke Province Alternating Duty Record Register,” it is ranked Senior First Rank. It is the Sannomiya of Kōzuke Province. However, as its patron clans declined, its influence waned from the medieval period onward. According to Ozaki Kizao’s “A Study of Ikaho Shrine,” during the early period of mountain worship the shrine was not located at its present site, and Ikaho Shrine existed as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=satomiya|1=UNKNOWN}} at the current site of Sannomiya Shrine. It is also said that the powerful Arima clan (Arima no Kimi) conducted the rites, and that the shrine was originally located in Arima, Shibukawa City, where Wakumikaho Shrine now stands. By at least the Heian period it was relocated to the Ikaho hot spring town and became the guardian deity of the hot springs. In the early modern period it was called “Onsen Shrine.” Its {{ill|lt=bettō|Q2900274|qid=Q2900274|1=Bettō}} was Onsen-ji Temple, founded during the {{ill|lt=Kan’ei era|Q1192913|qid=Q1192913|1=Kan'ei}}. In {{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|qid=Q7757|1=1873}} ({{ill|lt=Meiji era|Q3125096|qid=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 6), the shrine name was restored to “Ikaho Shrine.” In September {{ill|lt=19|Q2896|qid=Q2896|1=September 19}} of the same year, it became both a prefectural shrine and a village shrine. As for the shrine buildings, they were completely destroyed by a fire in March {{ill|lt=1878|Q7776|qid=Q7776|1=1878}} (Meiji 11). The subsidiary Onsen Shrine (formerly the Yakushi Hall of Iō-ji Temple), which had also been lost at the same time, was merged in {{ill|lt=1884|Q7819|qid=Q7819|1=1884}} (Meiji 17), and the current shrine buildings (temporary shrine) were rebuilt on that site, remaining to the present day. === Grounds === [[File:伊香保神社 Ikaho shrine - panoramio.jpg|thumb|right|200px|Haiden. On the right side of the photo is a pine tree planted by hand by [[Princess Tsunemiya Masako|Princess Masako of Tsunemiya]].]] The shrine is located at the top of the Ikaho Onsen stone stairway, which is 300 meters long and has 360 steps.{{Cite web|Japanese|title=Examples of Tourism Social Capital: The Stone Stairway of Ikaho Onsen|url=https://www.mlit.go.jp/sogoseisaku/region/kankosyakaisihon/83/1002.pdf|publisher=Ministry of Land, Infrastructure, Transport and Tourism|accessdate=2022-10-23}} In July {{ill|lt=1889|Q7831|qid=Q7831|1=1889}} (Meiji 22), {{ill|lt=Princess Masako of Tsunemiya|Q151930|qid=Q151930|1=Princess Masako Takeda}} planted two pine trees by hand in front of the shrine buildings, and a commemorative monument was erected. Treasures include a silver bell bestowed by Princess Masako, Kyoto dolls, and a framed calligraphy by {{ill|lt=Prince Arisugawa Takehito|Q875481|qid=Q875481|1=Prince Arisugawa Takehito}}.{{ill|lt=Tayama Katai|Q332975|qid=Q332975|1=Katai Tayama}} “Ikaho Guide, Revised Edition” (Japan Hot Spring Association Agency, Shōwa 5), p. 16 === Related shrines === {{Commonscat|Ikaho-jinja}} * {{ill|lt=Sannomiya Shrine (Yoshioka Town)|Q21655265|qid=Q21655265|lt=Sannomiya Shrine|1=Sannomiya Shrine (Yoshioka Town)}} (satomiya; former main shrine during the period of mountain worship; Sannomiya of Kōzuke Province) * {{ill|lt=Wakumikaho Shrine|Q21655266|qid=Q21655266|1=Wakaikaho Shrine}} (According to the theory of {{ill|lt=Ozaki Kizao|Q21698487|qid=Q21698487|1=Kisao Ozaki}}, when the Arima clan transferred the satomiya/Sannomiya Shrine, this shrine remained at the old site) === References === {{Reflist}} === Further reading === * {{Cite book|Japanese|author=Ozaki Kizao|chapter=A Study of Ikaho Shrine|title=Faith and Culture of Kōzuke Province|publisher=Ozaki Sensei Book Publication Society|date=1970-05}} * {{Cite book|Japanese|author=Ozaki Kizao|title=A Study of the Shrine Register of Kōzuke Province|publisher=Ozaki Sensei Book Publication Society|date=1974-12}} === Related topics === * {{ill|lt=Ikaho Shrine Plaque Incident|Q11381015|qid=Q11381015|1=Ikaho Shrine Plaque Incident}} === External links === * [https://www.city.shibukawa.lg.jp/kankou/ikahoonsen/streetsofikaho/p000202.html Ikaho Shrine] (Shibukawa City) == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Ikaho Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Ikaho Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Ikaho Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha in Kōzuke Province]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Kōzuke Province]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Gunma Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Gunma Prefecture]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Gunma Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Shibukawa, Gunma]] [[Category:渋川市の建築物]] [[Category:Kōzuke Province]] [[Category:Ikaho hot spring area]]
